meta.title: Causal loops — the dynamics primitive
meta.subject: Feedback loops — reinforcing (R) and balancing (B), the cognitive primitive of system dynamics

scenes.title: The dynamics primitive
scenes.tagline: Two kinds of feedback — and how to tell them apart
scenes.footnote: polarity glyphs · cycle closure · reinforcing vs balancing

beats.narration: Some arguments are not lists of components. They are cycles -- variables that drive each other in a loop.
A causal loop diagram shows that structure. Each edge carries a polarity. Each closed cycle is reinforcing or balancing. Today we walk both.
Polarity glyphs do the assertion. Cycle closure makes it a loop. The R or B label says which dynamics this loop produces.

scenes.heading: The docent flywheel — every step lifts the next

beats.narration: Start with what docent already does. Surveys feed films. Films feed judge runs. Judge runs distill into lessons. Lessons tighten the briefs. Better briefs make better surveys.
Each step pushes the next one up. Every arrow is a plus -- an increase here drives an increase there.
Five plus edges. Zero minus edges. The polarity count is even, so the loop is reinforcing -- the R label. Each turn of this cycle compounds the previous one.

scenes.heading: The thermostat — odd negative count, self-correcting

beats.narration: Now a different shape. A room gets hot. The thermostat turns on the AC. The AC removes heat. The room cools down -- which turns the AC off.
Watch the polarity. Hot drives AC on -- plus. AC on drives heat removed up -- plus. But heat removed drives temperature DOWN -- a minus.
One minus. Odd parity. That makes this a balancing loop -- a B. The cycle self-corrects: any push away from equilibrium produces a push back.

scenes.heading: Ice-albedo feedback — two minuses make a plus

beats.narration: A causal loop can be reinforcing even when most of its edges are negative. Here is climate's most-studied example.
Warming melts ice -- minus. Ice reflects sunlight -- plus. Reflection reduces what gets absorbed -- minus. Absorbed sunlight warms the planet -- plus.

Two minuses, two plusses. The two negative edges multiply to a positive -- warming begets warming. Even parity, reinforcing R. Climate models attribute roughly 25 percent of polar amplification to this single loop. This is why a planet can run away from itself.
Before the takeaway, name what the abstraction costs. A causal loop is a sketch, not an equation.
It assumes edges are linear. The polarity glyph cannot capture that ice melts ten times faster above zero than below it. At scale, the linearity breaks.

It assumes no delays. A real thermostat overshoots because the room's heat capacity lags the controller by something like sixty seconds. The loop label still reads B -- but the dynamics oscillate. At-scale failure: a control loop that hunts forever.

It assumes no saturation. Real predators do not breed past the prey supply -- the negative edge eventually clips. A reinforcing runaway crashes when it hits the wall. Failure mode: the loop diagram predicts runaway, the system breaks.

We chose readability over differential rigor. A differential equation captures all three. A causal loop trades that fidelity for one thing nothing else gives: an argument the viewer can see at a glance. The cost is that every loop is approximate -- and every reader should hold both.

scenes.heading: The takeaway
scenes.statement: Count the minuses along the loop — even parity compounds, odd parity self-corrects.

beats.narration: If you remember one thing about a feedback diagram, remember the parity rule. Even number of minus edges, the loop is reinforcing -- it compounds. Odd, balancing -- it self-corrects.

scenes.heading: What the loop tells you
points: The polarity glyph asserts direction — plus drives up, minus drives down.
A loop is reinforcing if its negative count is even; balancing if odd. Count the minuses.
Reinforcing loops compound — flywheels and runaways. Balancing loops self-correct — thermostats and predator-prey.
Two minuses make a plus — the structure, not any single arrow, names the dynamics.
Causal loops are sketches, not equations. They cannot model delay, saturation, or non-linear edges — use them to argue, then reach for differential equations to compute.

beats.narration: Polarity glyphs do the asserting. Plus means same direction; minus means opposite.
Count the minuses along the loop. Even count, reinforcing R. Odd count, balancing B.
Reinforcing loops are flywheels and runaways. Balancing loops are thermostats and predator-prey ecosystems.
And two minuses make a plus -- the parity, not the sign of any single arrow, is what tells you which dynamics the cycle produces.

Verdict: causal loops are sound for arguing structure, overstated for predicting behaviour. Their fragility is the linearity assumption; their cost is precision. The diagram cannot tell you the system will oscillate at point seven hertz -- only that something will. Use them to think; reach for the differential equation to compute.
